coredata.dc:title: Construction of Z–scheme 1D CdS nanorods/2D ultrathin CeO2 nanosheets toward enhanced photodegradation and hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Qiang
Cui, Yuchen
Zhang, Haonan
Wang, Shuaijun
Liu, Bin
Liu, Chenguang
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of semiconductor-based photocatalytic materials has been developed rapidly because of the wide application potential in solving environmental pollution and energy crises. In this study, a noval Z–scheme heterojunction by in situ assembly of two-dimensional (2D) ceria nanosheets and one-dimensional (1D) cadmium sulfide nanorods (CdS/CeO2) was constructed and employed for effective photocatalytic degradation of contaminant and hydrogen production under irradiation. The morphologies and physicochemical properties of as-synthesized nanostructures were analyzed by a series of characterizations. The optimized CdS/CeO2 showed more superior degradation efficiency of tetracycline than the most CdS-based materials as reported, and exhibited about 1.8 folds higher hydrogen evolution efficiency than pristine CdS. A direct Z–scheme charge transfer mechanism was proposed by combining radicals trapping experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance spectra, which accelerated the efficient transport of photo-induced charge carriers. The present work demonstrates that CdS/CeO2 nanomaterial with high photocatalytic performance has a promising application in the environmental treatment and green energy production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CdS/CeO2
Z−scheme heterojunction
Degradation
Hydrogen evolution
Mechanism

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21808243 and U22078362), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2017YFB0306600), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant Nos.17CX02061).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the synthesized catalysts.

(a) SEM, (b) TEM and (c–d) AFM images of CeO2, (e) SEM and (f) TEM images of CdS, (g) SEM and (h–j) TEM images of CdS/CeO2-15, (k) EDX spectrum and (l) the elemental mapping images of CdS/CeO2–15 composite.
XPS spectra of CeO2, CdS, and CdS/CeO2-15 composite: (a) survey spectrum, (b) Cd 3d, (c) Ce 3d, (d) S 2p, and (e) O 1s.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra, (b) evaluation of band–gap energy, (c) XPS valence band, and (d) band gap structure of the synthesized catalysts.

(a) Photocatalytic removal of TC, (b) kinetics curves, (c) kinetics values under different systems, (d) the stability of CdS/CeO2–15 for the degradation of TC, and (e–f) H2 evolution efficiency of CdS/CeO2–15 composite.
(a) Photocurrent, (b) EIS, (c) photoluminescence spectra, (d) degradation efficiency for TC under different scavengers, and (e–f) EPR spectra of the synthesized catalysts.
Proposed charge separation mechanism for CdS/CeO2–15 composite, (a) type–II and (b) Z–scheme under visible light irradiation.
The schematic explanation for formation process of CdS/CeO2 nanohybrid.

head.ce:title: Construction of Z–scheme 1D CdS nanorods/2D ultrathin CeO2 nanosheets toward enhanced photodegradation and hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qinghai Normal University, Xining 810008, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qinghai Normal University, Xining 810008, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Power Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy and Power Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of semiconductor-based photocatalytic materials has been developed rapidly because of the wide application potential in solving environmental pollution and energy crises. In this study, a noval Z–scheme heterojunction by in situ assembly of two-dimensional (2D) ceria nanosheets and one-dimensional (1D) cadmium sulfide nanorods (CdS/CeO2) was constructed and employed for effective photocatalytic degradation of contaminant and hydrogen production under irradiation. The morphologies and physicochemical properties of as-synthesized nanostructures were analyzed by a series of characterizations. The optimized CdS/CeO2 showed more superior degradation efficiency of tetracycline than the most CdS-based materials as reported, and exhibited about 1.8 folds higher hydrogen evolution efficiency than pristine CdS. A direct Z–scheme charge transfer mechanism was proposed by combining radicals trapping experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance spectra, which accelerated the efficient transport of photo-induced charge carriers. The present work demonstrates that CdS/CeO2 nanomaterial with high photocatalytic performance has a promising application in the environmental treatment and green energy production.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, antibiotics have been widely used with the continuous expansion of animal husbandry and aquaculture. The environmental pollution and ecological toxicological effect of tetracycline (TC), as an important and non–biodegradable antibiotic, have become a serious problem for the whole world [1]. It remains a huge challenge to develop new and effective remediation methods for TC removal. Solar–driven photocatalysis has been proven to be an emerging technology with great potential for eliminating global environmental and energy crises in the future [2]. However, many reports have shown that most of the single–component semiconductor materials show a low contaminant removal efficiency during photocatalytic degradation, and numerous studies on the construction of heterojunction photocatalysts have been carried out in recent years [3-5]. It has been generally accepted that well–matched nanostructures and close interfacial contact are more conducive to promoting charge transfer, thus improving the photocatalytic degradation performance [6]. Hence, it is urgent to design a series of photocatalysts with well–defined sizes and interfaces to realize the treatment of organic pollutants by using visible light irradiation.

As a kind of versatile semiconductor photocatalytic materials, metal sulfide has been considered as a novel photocatalytic material owing to their suitable bandgap and remarkable physiochemical stability [7]. Particularly, cadmium sulfide (CdS) with band gap of 2.4 eV has gained more and more research attention because of the wide visible light response, suitable band gap structure, and unique one–dimensional (1D) nanostructures [8]. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic performance of pristine CdS was seriously hindered by the low quantum efficiency and serious photocorrosion, which also restricted its practical application [9,10]. Undoubtedly, 1D CdS nanorods have larger specific surface area and higher length–to–diameter ratio than other bulk materials, and this nanostructure was extremely beneficial to broaden absorption range of visible light and promote interfacial charge transfer along their axes, thus exhibiting excellent catalytic activity [11]. So far, many studies have reported a strategy to promote the photocatalytic activity of 1D CdS by combining with other semiconductor materials to construct heterojunction systems. For example, Liu et al. [12] designed a unique visible–light–driven 2D/1D MgIn2S4/CdS composite for wastewater treatment by a simple hydrothermal approach, and 95.98% of oxytetracycline was decompose within 60 min under irradiation. The enhanced charge transfer efficiency was illuminated by the intimate contact of different nanostructures and the construction of heterostructure with matched band gaps. Meng et al. [13] also fabricated noble metal–free NiSx/CdS nanohybrids based on the electron transfer strategy with efficient separation and transportation of electron–hole, and demonstrated superior performance of photocatalytic hydrogen evaluation and contamination removal in water. Therefore, it can be perceived that construction of 1D CdS-based nanocomposites shows a great potential for exploiting new visible light driven photocatalyst.

As a typical n-type semiconductor material and rare earth oxides with rich earth reserves, ceria (CeO2) with broad band gap (about 2.8 ~ 3.1 eV) was considered as a novel photocatalytic material because of its wide bandgap energy, high stability, outstanding optical and catalytic properties, and cost–effectiveness. It was reported that the band gap structure and electron transfer processes of materials were greatly affected by their tunable morphology, crystal structure and particle size [14]. Up to now, many techniques have been developed to synthesize CeO2 nanomaterials with various morphologies, such as 1D nanorods/nanowires/nanotubes [15,16], 2D nanocubes/nanosheets [17-19], and 3D nanospheres/hollow nanosphere [20-22]. Impressively, ceria with 2D thin–layer structure exhibited more effective photoactivity by coupling with co-catalysts because of the dual function of catalyst and supporter. For instance, Sultana et al. [23] have reported a CeO2–based 2D–2D nanocomposite for RhB dye decolorization under illumination, and the remarkable photocatalytic performance was primarily due to the efficient charge transport through Z–scheme between BiOI and CeO2 nanosheets. So far, many previous reports about the combination of CdS and CeO2 have explained the appropriate band structure between them. For example, Xu et al. [24] and Zheng et al. [21] have synthesized CdS@CeO2 core–shell material for photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants by solvothermal method and template–assisted approach, respectively. Although they all exhibited considerable photocatalytic activity, most of them conformed to the traditional Type Ⅱ charge transfer mechanism. The Z–scheme mechanism was of superior photocatalytic properties compared with traditional modes, which could not only enhance the charge transfer and separation, but also retain a strong redox potential [25,26]. Simultaneously, it has been commonly accepted that 1D and 2D materials have gradually become the research focus in terms of photocatalysis due to their unique dimensionality–dependent advantages [11,27]. Hence, the integration of 1D CdS nanorods and 2D CeO2 nanosheets into composites with direct Z–scheme mode could provide new enriched structural and high charge transfer efficiency, which was beneficial to promote the photocatalytic performance.

Herein, a series of CdS/CeO2 photocatalysts were fabricated by a facile solvothermal approach to successfully immobilize 1D CdS nanorods on the surface of 2D CeO2 nanosheets, which demonstrated excellent photocatalytic performance for TC degradation and hydrogen generation under light irradiation because the heterojunction formed between the two nanostructures might greatly inhibit the recombination of photogenerated carriers. Unlike the traditional transfer mode, a direct Z–scheme transfer mechanism in the CdS/CeO2 composite was verified by combining quenching experiments and EPR technique.

CeO2 nanosheets were prepared according to the procedures described by Dai et al [19]. Typically, 2.78 g of cerium nitrate hexahydrate and 1.50 g of ammonium bicarbonate were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water, respectively. Then, the two suspensions were mixed rapidly and stirred constantly for 30 min, and statically aged at 303 K for 24 h. The precipitates were separated by filtration, washed with ultrapure water three times and fully dried at 353 K in an oven. Finally, the belt–like CeO2 nanosheets were obtained by calcination at 773 K for 4 h in air.

The CdS/CeO2 was prepared by the solvothermal process illustrated in Scheme 1 . 20 mg of CeO2 powder was dispersed in 40 mL of ethylenediamine and sonicated for 20 min. Then, 0.537 g cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate and 0.398 g thiourea were added into the above solution and continuously stirred for 60 min. Subsequently, this solution was transferred to a 50 mL autoclave and maintained at 423 K for 24 h. After the solvothermal treatment, the precipitates denoted as CdS/CeO2-15 were collected via centrifugation and washed, finally placed in a drying oven at 353 K overnight. In the above process, change the amount of CeO2 nanosheets (15, 30 or 60 mg) to obtain CdS/CeO2-5%, CdS/CeO2-10%, CdS/CeO2-20% composites, respectively, where the number refers to the molar ratio of Cd/Ce. Pristine CdS nanorods was also prepared by following the same procedure without CeO2 nanosheets. The details for materials characterization, degradation experiment and analytical methods were described in Supporting Information.

For each photodegradation experiment, a typical reaction of tetracycline decomposition (50 mL, 10 mg/L) was carried out in a 50 mL quartz reactor, and 20 mg of catalyst was added with a constant stirring of 400 rpm for 30 min in the dark in order to establish an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then the degradation reaction was initiated by irradiating under a 300 W Xenon lamp with a UV cutoff filter (λ > 420 nm). The distance between the lamp and liquid level in the reactor was 15 cm. At given intervals of degradation, 2 mL of suspension was withdrawn and an aliquot of 1 M methanol as a quenching reagent was immediately added to prevent further reaction, which was then filtered through 0.22 μm membrane filters to remove suspended catalyst for analysis. After each reaction, the used catalyst was separated, washed dried to perform subsequent photoreaction cycles. This process was repeated several times to test the reusability of CdS/CeO2. In addition, radical quenching experiments were carried out by adding quenching agents into the reaction solution to evaluate the role of radicals in the degradation process. All the experiments were repeated three times and the reported data represented the average of the triplicates to ensure accurate data acquisition and interpretation.

The photocatalytic performance was also investigated by monitoring the hydrogen evolution, which was performed in a closed on-line photocatalytic system. Briefly, 20 mg of catalyst powder was added to an aqueous methanol solution (25 mL) and sonicated for 30 min. Before the visible light irradiations, the reaction equipment was evacuated thoroughly by a vacuum pump to remove air. The solution was then irradiated by a 300 W Xe lamp with a cut-off filter under magnetic stirring to produce H2. The evolved hydrogen was analyzed by an online gas chromatograph (GC7920, Techcomp, TCD detector, carrier gas: N2, 5A molecular sieve column).

The XRD patterns were analyzed to investigate the phases and compositions of CeO2, CdS, and CdS/CeO2 samples. As presented in Fig. 1 , the diffraction peaks of 28.6°, 33.1°, 47.6°, 56.4°, 59.2°, and 69.5° could be found, which were quite related with the (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), and (400) planes of pristine CeO2 with typical fluorite structure (JCPDF: 43-1002), respectively [28]. For CdS, the typical diffraction peaks at 25.0° (100), 26.6° (002), 28.4° (101), 36.7° (102), 43.7° (110), 47.8° (103), 51.9° (112), and 66.8° (203) crystal facets could be well indexed to hexagonal wurtzite–structure CdS [29], demonstrating a successful fabrication of CdS. In addition, the resultant CdS/CeO2 composites exhibited a coincident characteristic diffraction peaks corresponding to both CeO2 and CdS, and no additional impurity were observed, implying the successful growth of CdS nanorods on CeO2 nanosheets. Additionally, the diffraction intensities of CdS gradually enhanced with the increase of mass content, and there was no interference to the peaks position of CeO2, indicating an unchanged crystal structure after the integration of nanostructures. Therefore, it could be concluded that CeO2 nanosheets did not obstruct the crystal growth of CdS nanorods.

The surface morphology and micro–structure of CeO2, CdS, and CdS/CeO2–15 nanohybrid was observed by SEM, TEM, and HRTEM. Fig. 2 a and b shown that pristine CeO2 possessed a regular sheet structure with the width dimension of 1 ~ 1.5 μm and length size of 2 ~ 8 μm. In particular, a laminar structure of CeO2 was found by AFM, and the uniform thickness of 2D ultrathin nanosheet was approximately 3.8 nm (Fig. 2c and d). This ultrathin material could facilitate the absorption of visible light and the migration of carrier [30]. Fig. 2e and f shown the SEM and TEM pictures of 1D CdS nanorods, and it was clear that large amounts of nanorods with diameters of around 20 nm were randomly placed into a bulk. It was worth noting that a 1D/2D heterostructure of CdS/CeO2–15 nanohybrid was formed by the integration that CdS nanorods were tightly anchored on the surface of CeO2 nanosheets (Fig. 2g–i). The HRTEM image (Fig. 2j) of CdS/CeO2–15 composite also indicated that the interface between 2D CeO2 and 1D CdS was in close contact, which could accelerate the charge transfer and thus promote the photocatalytic performance [31]. Meanwhile, N2 sorption results (Fig. S1) suggested that CdS/CeO2–15 composite has the characteristic of mesoporous with a typical type-IV isotherm, and the BET surface area (Table S1) was obviously increased compared with pure CdS, which might also provide more active sites and contribute to superior catalytic performance [32]. Besides, the lattice spacings over CdS/CeO2–15 sample of 0.31 and 0.33 nm were observed, which could be attributed to (111) planes of CeO2 and (002) plane of CdS, respectively [33,34]. Finally, the EDX elemental mapping images were analyzed to reveal the spatial elemental composition and distribution and the results were shown in Fig. 2k and l, the uniform distribution of Ce, O, Cd, and S elements in the CdS/CeO2–15 composite indicated the successful synthesis of the 1D CdS/2D CeO2 heterojunction.

The surface elemental compositions and chemical valences of CeO2, CdS, and CdS/CeO2-15 composite were investigated by XPS analysis. The binding energies (BEs) obtained by XPS characterization were modified by taking the BE of C 1s as a standard [35]. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the full survey spectrum depicted the existence of Cd, S, Ce, and O in CdS/CeO2–15 nanohybrid. In the Cd 3d high resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 3b), the characteristic orbital splits of Cd 2p5/2 and Cd 2p3/2 of Cd2+ species were located at 405.3 and 412.0 eV, respectively [36]. Fig. 3c shown the XPS spectrum for Ce 3d energy levels, and two sets of signals corresponding to the 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 photoemissions were observed. The peaks at BEs 916.7, 907.3, 903.7, and 900.9 eV were assignable to Ce 3d3/2 and the Ce 3d5/2 features at BEs 898.4, 888.8, 884.8, and 882.4 eV. Additionally, the signals at BE = 884.8 and 903.7 eV were classified as surface Ce3+, while others were attributed to surface Ce4+ species [37]. These results could be explained that Ce existed in mixed oxidation species of Ce3+ and Ce4+ on the surface of catalysts. Meanwhile, in comparison to pristine CeO2 and CdS, the two peaks of Cd 3d in the CdS/CeO2-15 composite shifted to higher binding energies and the Ce 3d states migrated to lower binding energies, indicating the formation of strong chemical interaction and heterojunction between CeO2 and CdS, which was beneficial to accelerate the separation of photogenerated e-–h+ and thus improve the photocatalytic performance [38,39]. For S element, the spectrum in Fig. 3d included two individual peaks at 162.8 (S 2p1/2) and 161.6 (S 2p3/2) assigned to the spin-orbit doublet of S2− [40]. Fig. 3e shown that O 1s spectrum could split into two peaks at 531.8 and 529.4 eV corresponding to the characteristic orbital splits of lattice oxide oxygen and chemisorbed oxygen [41].

The light absorption property of CeO2, CdS, and CdS/CeO2 samples were studied by using UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy technique. It could be seen from Fig. 4 a that pristine CdS with a maximum absorption edge around 540 nm exhibited a stronger absorption than pure CeO2 (456 nm). Moreover, all the CdS/CeO2 composites exhibited an absorption band edge similar to that of CdS, which indicates that CdS nanorods decorated the surface of CeO2 nanosheets, but not incorporated into the lattice of CeO2. Notably, the optical properties of as–synthesized CdS/CeO2 composites shown a significant change with an enhanced absorption capability in the range below 550 nm after the modification of CdS on surface of CeO2, which suggested that there was a close interaction between CeO2 and CdS. The maximum absorption was found in CeO2 with 15% CdS, and excessive CdS could reduce the active sites exposed on the surface of CeO2 nanosheets and affect the migration of photo–generated electrons under irradiations, thus impeding the photocatalytic performance. Besides, according to the Kubelka-Munk equation, the band gaps of CeO2, CdS, and CdS/CeO2–15 were obtained to be 2.72, 2.27, and 2.30 eV, respectively (Fig. 4b). A significant decline of bandgap energy was observed for CdS/CeO2-15 photocatalyst as compared to CeO2, implying a great potential to produce e-–h+ pairs under light irradiation.

The valence band (VB) spectra of CeO2 and CdS were further measured by the XPS-VB technique to determine the energy-band structure and the results were shown in Fig. 4c. The obtained VB positions for CeO2 and CdS were at 2.37 and 0.90 eV, respectively. Furthermore, the related conduction band (CB) potential of CeO2 and CdS were calculated to be-0.35 and-1.39 eV vs NHE by combining with their band-gap energies [42]. The resulting band structures were illustrated in Fig. 4d. It was clearly indicated that CB and VB edge potential position of CdS were both more negative than those of CeO2, which could lead to the formation of a well-matched band structure. In addition, this band structure could effectively suppress charge recombination of photoexcited electron–hole pairs, thus lead to superior catalytic performance.

The visible light enhanced photocatalytic degradation ability of CdS/CeO2 composites were investigated by the removal of TC as the pollutant. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the time before zero represented the adsorption equilibrium between the catalysts and TC in the dark. The photocatalytic activity of pure CeO2 and CdS were compared under irradiation and the degradation efficiency for TC in 30 min were 12.8% and 72.4%, respectively. The low removal efficiency was mainly due to the poor absorption for visible light and the fatal recombination of photogenerated e-–h+. The photocatalytic performance was obviously enhanced after the integration of CeO2 and CdS, and CdS/CeO2–15 with the highest activity was capable to decompose TC up to 98.0%. As far as we know, the superior degradation efficiency was higher than most reported CdS–based materials in the treatment of non–biodegradable pollutants (Table S2). The outstanding photocatalytic activity might be caused by the existence of abundant heterojunctions between CeO2 and CdS [43]. Besides, the 1D/2D nanostructures with intimate interfacial contact enable efficient charge transport across the surfaces, further promoting the separation of charge carriers. Meanwhile, the apparent rate constant value for TC removal of CdS/CeO2–15 was higher (Fig. 5b and c), about 2.9 and 33.2 times than that of CdS and CeO2, respectively. With the increasing content of CeO2, the rate constant increased initially but then reduced, which was consistent with previous literatures [44,45]. Therefore, the appropriate doping content of CeO2 was an important factor to determine the photocatalytic degradation activity. The fact that insufficient CeO2 reduced the reaction rate gradually could be ascribed to the obvious agglomeration of CdS nanorods on the surface of CeO2 nanosheets, which would weaken the absorption of visible light and might also be converted into charge recombination centers [46]. Meanwhile, the reason for negative effects of superfluous CeO2 might be due to the shielding and blocking effect of active sites [47].

The mineralization degree of TC by CdS/CeO2–15 composite was further investigated by TOC measurement. As shown in Fig. S2, the TOC removal efficiency enhanced with the increase of degradation time, indicating the continuous advancement of mineralization process. Remarkably, about 63.7% mineralization efficiency of TC was achieved by CdS/CeO2–15 composite under 30 min irradiation, which was much higher than that of single CdS (26.5%). The high mineralization efficiency indicated that the integration of 2D CeO2 nanosheeets and 1D CdS nanorods exhibited excellent oxidation capacity. In addition, the stability of CdS/CeO2–15 photocatalyst was further investigated in six successive TC degradation experiments under the consistent conditions (Fig. 5d). Obviously, the CdS/CeO2–15 composite exhibited extraordinary photocatalytic performance, and the removal rate of TC was still not less than 90% after six cycles, indicating that the formation of heterostructures between 1D CdS and 2D CeO2 could effectively facilitate the activity and stability of photocatalyst. To further verify the stability of synthesized catalyst, the concentration of leaching Cd2+ in the solution after photocatalytic reaction was detected (Fig. S3). During each cycle, the leaching amounts of Cd2+ over CdS/CeO2-15 composite were 2.43, 2.73, and 2.62 mg/L, respectively, which was far lower than that of pure CdS (18.66 mg/L). Hence, the integration of CeO2 and CdS could effectively avoid photocorrosion effect of CdS. Besides, the effects of several key parameters such as initial TC concentrations, pH, and catalyst dosage on TC degradation were presented and discussed in Fig. S4 (Supplementary information)

In order to further confirm the application potential, the hydrogen evolution rate of CdS/CeO2–15 was studied in aqueous methanol solution under illumination for 4 h. As displayed in Fig. 5e and f, the photocatalytic H2 production efficiency of pristine CdS was compared and only 38.4 μmol·g−1·h−1 H2 could be produced. Notably, CdS/CeO2–15 exhibited the greatest H2 evolution efficiency (70.1 μmol·g−1·h−1), which was about 1.8 and 33.4 times higher than that of CdS and CeO2, respectively. The main reason for this result was attributed to the negative CB edge of CdS/CeO2–15, suggesting the superior reduction ability [48]. In general, the effective integration of CdS and CeO2 was beneficial to the improvement of redox efficiency.

It was well known that the generation and separation rate of the photoexcited e-–h+ pairs were vital factors for photocatalytic activity. Therefore, the charge transfer process of CeO2, CdS, and CdS/CeO2–15 was further investigated by transient photocurrent response, EIS, and PL. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the CdS/CeO2–15 composite have the strongest photocurrent response intensity during four on-off periods, and was about 1.4 and 3.4 folds larger than that of pure CdS and CeO2, respectively, which indicated that the as-synthesized composite could generate more charge carriers and more efficient separation and transfer than the two individual units under visible light irradiation [49]. Additionally, a similarly trend could also be observed in EIS curves. (Fig. 6b). It was generally accepted that a smaller semicircle diameter represented a lower charge transfer resistance, which indicated that the recombination of photo–induced electrons and holes was successfully prevented [50]. The CdS/CeO2–15 composite shown the smallest semicircle diameter, indicating the highest charge transfer efficiency, which was helpful to improve the photocatalytic performance. Besides, the broad band and high peak intensity of photoluminescence curves as shown in Fig. 6c confirmed a fast recombination of charges, so the CdS/CeO2–15 composite has lower recombination probability of the photogenerated electron-hole compared with pure CeO2 and CdS.

To ascertain the dominant active radicals associated with the removal of TC over CdS/CeO2–15 composite, quenching experiments were performed in the existence of BQ, IPA, and AO, which served as scavengers of •O2 −, •OH, and h+, respectively [51]. As described in Fig. 6d, significant inhibitory effect on the removal efficiency of TC was found after adding different scavengers, suggesting that •O2 −, •OH, and h+ were appeared in the degradation process. Concretely, the removal efficiency of TC was suppressed from 98.0% to 44.4%, 86.3%, and 83.4% after adding BQ, IPA, and AO, respectively, and the highest inhibition effect was observed in the existence of BQ. This result implied that •O2 − was the key oxidizing species responsible for the TC degradation reaction.

To further identify radical species produced by CdS and CdS/CeO2–15 composite under visible light irradiation, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) technique was carried out. As presented in Fig. 6e and f, no obvious characteristic signals were found under dark condition, implying that •O2 − and •OH were produced only in the photocatalytic process. Notably, a classical four–line spectrum with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 was monitored for CdS and CdS/CeO2–15 composite after irradiation for 5 min (Fig. 6e), which was the signal related to the DMPO-OH adduct, indicating the generation of •OH under visible light irradiation [52]. Moreover, the stronger EPR signal intensity suggested that CdS/CeO2–15 composite could produce or convert more •OH radical than CdS under the same illumination conditions. Similarly, the signal peak of DMPO−•O2 − in Fig. 6f reflected that more •O2 − radical was generated in the CdS/CeO2–15 photocatalytic system [53]. In general, the integration of 1D CdS nanorods and 2D CeO2 nanosheets into composites significantly improved the electron–hole pairs separation compared with the single component and generated more oxidizing species, which greatly promoted the photocatalytic performance.

On the basis of all results given above, the two possible charge transfer mechanism such as Type Ⅱ and Z–Scheme were proposed, as demonstrated in Fig. 7 . In the Type Ⅱ transfer mechanism (Fig. 7a), the electrons of both CdS and CeO2 under light irradiation were excited and subsequently rapidly transmitted from the CB of CdS to the CB of CeO2 due to the fact that the CB edge potential of CdS was much more negative than that of CeO2. Meanwhile, the same amount of photo–induced holes (h+) could also transfer from the VB of CeO2 to the VB of CdS. Nevertheless, it was difficult for the electrons accumulated on the CB of CeO2 to interact with surface adsorbed oxygen molecule (O2) to generate •O2 − because the CB edge potential of CeO2 (−0.35 eV) was almost equal to the O2/•O2 − with a redox potential of-0.33 eV. This was incompatible with the conclusion of radical quenching experiment that the •O2 − was the main oxidizing species in the TC removal process. Hence, the plausible photocatalytic mechanism over CdS/CeO2–15 composite under simulated solar irradiation might be the Z–Scheme charge carrier transfer mode (Fig. 7b) rather than the Type Ⅱ transfer mechanism (Fig. 7a). The photoexcited electrons located on the CB of CeO2 were directly consumed by the holes on the VB of CdS. The photo–generated electrons on the CB of CdS could react with O2 into •O2 −, some •O2 − could be further converted to hydroxyl radicals (•OH), both of which could be used to degrade pollutants. Meanwhile, the photo–generated electrons could also reduce H+ to form H2. In addition, the photo–generated h+ on the VB of CeO2 with strong oxidizing capacity could directly contribute towards oxidation degradation of TC. As a result, the photo–generated e-–h+ pair in CdS/CeO2–15 composite was not only separated effectively, but also retained a strong redox potential, which was conducive to the improvement of photocatalytic activity.

In summary, CdS/CeO2 heterojunction with Z–Scheme carrier transfer mechanism was first successfully prepared by a one-step solvothermal approach and used as an efficient photocatalyst for TC degradation and H2 evolution in this work. A close interfacial contact was constructed by in situ growth of 1D CdS nanorods on the 2D CeO2 nanosheets as a substrate, which not only improved the visible light response, but also greatly enhanced the rapid charge transfer. The optimal photocatalyst CdS/CeO2–15 exhibited excellent catalytic performance with 98.0% degradation efficiency for TC and high hydrogen evolution rate of 70.1 μmol·g−1·h−1 under illumination. Meanwhile, the effect of several key operation parameters such as catalyst dosage, PH, and initial concentration were systematically investigated, and CdS/CeO2–15 composite not only demonstrated remarkable reusability with TC degradation efficiency still higher than 90% after six cycles, but also shown satisfactory stability with lower Cr2+ leaching than CdS in the cycle experiments. The reaction mechanism was also confirmed with oxidizing species including •O2 −, •OH, and h+, and •O2 − was identified as the main active specie responsible for the degradation process. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of as–synthesized sample was attributed to the effective separation of photogenerated electron–hole pairs, as evidenced by a series of photoelectrochemical characterization such as EIS, PL, and transient photocurrent responses. This present work provides a synthesis strategy of photocatalytic materials with multidimensional nanostructures and environmentally friendly applications.

Qiang Gao: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Yuchen Cui: Data curation, Visualization, Formal analysis. Haonan Zhang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Shuaijun Wang: Investigation, Visualization. Bin Liu: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Chenguang Liu: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21808243 and U22078362), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2017YFB0306600), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant Nos.17CX02061).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2021.119116.

ce:section.ce:para:
